overview.body: This is a MASTER unit! You'll copy your Lab 1.2 code and add nested decisions. When a player reaches Houston, they get a second choice: continue to Galveston for the beach, or stay put. This is an if statement inside another if statement — decisions within decisions!

mentorQuote.quote: This is where it gets interesting! Nested if statements let you build decision trees — like a 'choose your own adventure' book. If you reach Houston AND you have miles left AND you want to continue... then you can try for Galveston. That's three levels of logic working together!

objectives: Write a nested if inside the Houston branch to ask about Galveston
Use the or operator to accept both 'y' and 'Y'
Set a status variable (WINNER, SHORT, DETOUR, STOPPED, ERROR) in every branch
Display a final message based on the status variable
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapter 3: Decision Structures and Boolean Logic

namedConstants.name: SAT_TO_CORPUS
namedConstants.description: Distance from San Antonio to Corpus Christi (150 miles)

namedConstants.name: SAT_TO_HOUSTON
namedConstants.description: Distance from San Antonio to Houston (200 miles)

namedConstants.name: SAT_TO_AUSTIN
namedConstants.description: Distance from San Antonio to Austin (80 miles)

namedConstants.name: HOU_TO_GALVESTON
namedConstants.description: Distance from Houston to Galveston (50 miles) — back from Lab 1.1!

programRequirements: Your program MUST start from your Lab 1.2 code
Your program MUST have a nested if inside the Houston branch
Your program MUST use the or operator to accept 'y' or 'Y'
Your program MUST set distance and status in every branch
Your program MUST display a final message based on status
Your program MUST include your docstring with name, abc123, section, and description

rows.task: Copy 1.2, add constant, add tracking vars
The big one: nested if with Galveston
Corpus, Austin, Invalid with new vars
Run end-to-end test, verify all 4 Houston outcomes plus other paths, submit to CodeGrade

timeGuide.strategy: Draw it out! Before coding, sketch the decision tree on paper. Houston alone has 4 possible outcomes: didn't make it, made it but stayed, made it and reached Galveston, or fell short of Galveston. Visualizing helps you track all the branches.

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here's how your finished program fits together. You're starting from Lab 1.2 — the [NEW] and [REPLACED] tags show what changes this unit.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- CONSTANTS ----
SAT_TO_CORPUS = 150
SAT_TO_HOUSTON = 200
SAT_TO_AUSTIN = 80
HOU_TO_GALVESTON = 50    [NEW-CP1]

# ---- MAIN PROGRAM ----
def main():
│
│── Welcome banner, player name, roll
│── miles_remaining = miles_rolled      [NEW-CP1]
│── distance = 0                  [NEW-CP1]
│
│── Display menu, get choice
│
│── if choice == 1 (Corpus):            [REPLACED-CP3]
│       check miles_remaining >= 0
│       set status WINNER or SHORT
│       set distance
│
│── elif choice == 2 (Houston):         [REPLACED-CP2]
│       check miles_remaining >= 0
│       if made it:
│       │   ask 'Continue to Galveston? (y/n)'
│       │   if answer == 'y' or answer == 'Y':
│       │       check remaining vs HOU_TO_GALVESTON
│       │       status = WINNER or SHORT
│       │   else:
│       │       status = STOPPED
│       else:
│           status = SHORT
│       set distance
│
│── elif choice == 3 (Austin):          [REPLACED-CP3]
│       check miles_remaining >= 0
│       status = DETOUR or SHORT
│       set distance
│
│── else (invalid):                     [REPLACED-CP3]
│       status = ERROR
│
│── Display TRIP SUMMARY
│── if status == 'WINNER': ...          [NEW-CP4]
│── elif status == 'SHORT': ...
│── elif status == 'DETOUR': ...
│── else: ...

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

content.body: A foundation with tracking variables that will accumulate totals as the player travels through multiple cities.
Tracking variables that accumulate values across a program are everywhere in real software: shopping carts, game scores, step counters. Learning to initialize and update them is fundamental.

items: Copy your Lab 1.2 code and save as lab-1-3.py
Update your docstring with the new lab number and description
Add the Galveston constant with your other constants: HOU_TO_GALVESTON = 50

content.title: Design Note: Galveston Is (Almost) Impossible
content.body: With the current constraints, the maximum roll is 20, giving 200 miles. Houston is 200 miles away, and Galveston is another 50 miles past that. Reaching Galveston is mathematically impossible with a normal roll — and that's intentional. Most games have outcomes that are hard or impossible to reach. You still need to code the WINNER path because CodeGrade will test it by sending rolls higher than 20. To test it yourself, temporarily enter a roll of 25 or higher.

references.description: Shows a nested if — an if inside another if. Study the indentation carefully. You're building on Lab 1.2 patterns while preparing for the nested structure in Checkpoint 2.

content.quote: Quick aside from QA. Notice the prompt says 'roll (1-20)' but nothing actually validates. Type 25 and you'll travel 250 miles. Type -5 and the math goes sideways. This is a real vulnerability, and it's intentional for now. In Chapter 5 you'll write a function called get_valid_int() that closes this hole and refuses bad input. Until then, the door is wide open. Notice when you're getting away with something — that instinct is what makes a good developer.

rows: Wrong initial value for tracking vars
distance starts at 0; miles_remaining starts at miles_rolled
NameError when you try to use it in Checkpoint 2
Placement matters: tracking vars must be initialized before the if-elif-else chain

content.body: Step 1: Add debug lines after your new variables:

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE AFTER TESTING
    print(f'DEBUG: miles_remaining = {miles_remaining}')
    print(f'DEBUG: distance = {distance}')

content.body: Step 2: Run with roll 15. Verify:

content.title: Delete debug lines
content.body: Delete debug lines before moving to Checkpoint 2.
The heart of this assignment: if the player reaches Houston, they can choose to continue to Galveston. This is an if inside an if — a nested if.

Nested decisions are how programs make sense of the real world. Real decisions aren't flat — they branch. Reach Houston? Then ask about Galveston. Loaded payment info? Then show the checkout button. The pattern of "if a particular thing happened, ask a follow-up question" lives at the heart of every non-trivial program you'll ever write.

references.description: Shows nested if — the inner if only runs when the outer if is true. Study how the indentation levels work. That's exactly what you need here.
Shows the or operator in conditions. The pattern you need: choice == 'y' or choice == 'Y' — you must repeat the full comparison on both sides.

content.quote: When I first learned the or operator, I kept writing 'if choice == "y" or "Y":' and wondering why it always ran. You need the full comparison on both sides: 'choice == "y" or choice == "Y"'. Trust me, this trips everyone up at first!

content.body: Replace your Houston branch (elif choice == 2) with nested logic. The structure is:

items: Check if made it to Houston (miles_remaining >= SAT_TO_HOUSTON)
If yes: subtract SAT_TO_HOUSTON from miles_remaining, set distance, then ask 'Continue to Galveston? (y/n): '
If they say y/Y: check if miles_remaining >= HOU_TO_GALVESTON — set status WINNER or SHORT accordingly
If they say anything else: set status to 'STOPPED'
If didn't make Houston: set status to 'SHORT', set distance to SAT_TO_HOUSTON

rows: Always True! Need: choice == 'y' or choice == 'Y'
Nested code must be indented further than the outer if
Must subtract SAT_TO_HOUSTON before checking if you can reach Galveston

content.title: Print Order Rule
content.body: Only print the final outcome ONCE, after all decisions are made. If the player chooses Houston, do not print a Houston arrival message before the Galveston prompt — the prompt comes first, then a single result printed after the if-elif-else fully resolves. Two prints for one game = wrong.
Test all 4 Houston outcomes:

items: Roll 20, choice 2, 'y': Made Houston, reached Galveston → WINNER
Roll 15, choice 2, 'y': Made Houston, short of Galveston → SHORT
Nested if inside the Houston branch
Logic handling y/Y for continue, anything else for stop

content.body: Updated Corpus, Austin, and Invalid branches that set distance and status so the final summary has accurate data.

Branch consistency is invisible until something breaks. If even one branch forgets to set status, the Trip Summary crashes with a NameError the moment a player picks that path — possibly after they've already played for a minute. Defensive programming means every branch leaves the program in a valid state, every time.
For each remaining branch, ensure you set both tracking variables:

content.content: About the invalid choice row: When the player types something other than 1, 2, or 3, your else clause needs to set both variables explicitly. Don't leave them undefined — the Trip Summary will crash with a NameError if distance or status isn't set.

content.title: Status Values
items: SHORT — Ran out of miles before the destination
DETOUR — Made it to Austin (no beach there!)

references.description: Shows setting different variables in each branch of an if-elif-else. Same pattern here — each destination sets its own status and distance.

rows: 'Winner' vs 'WINNER' — be consistent, Python is case-sensitive
status stays undefined, causing a NameError in the summary

content.body: Step 1: Add a debug line at the END of the full if-elif-else block (right before the summary):

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE AFTER TESTING
    print(f'DEBUG: distance = {distance}, status = {status}')

content.body: Step 2: Test each branch and verify distance and status are set correctly for every path.

content.title: Delete the debug line
content.body: Delete the debug line before moving to Checkpoint 4.
An updated trip summary showing total distance, plus a final message that changes based on the status variable.

The closing message is the last thing the player sees. It transforms a successful program into a satisfying experience — or it doesn't. Every status code you set in the previous checkpoints feeds into this one moment. The work you did making sure every branch sets status correctly pays off here, when the player gets exactly the right reaction to their outcome.

content.quote: I obsess over closing messages. The player just spent real time on your game — their last impression is what they tell their friends about. A generic 'Thanks for playing!' wastes that moment. A status-aware ending rewards their specific outcome: the winner gets a celebration, the loser gets encouragement, the wanderer gets a knowing wink. Same code, five emotional payoffs. That's craft.

items: Update the Trip Summary to show "Total Distance" using distance
After the summary, add an if-elif-else that checks the status variable and prints a matching closing message

content.body: Messages to display for each status:

rows: Suggestion to try a beach destination next time

references.description: Shows if-elif-else with string comparisons. Same pattern here — checking status against different string values to print different messages.

content.output:
Heading to Corpus Christi!
Distance: 150 miles

You made it to Corpus Christi!
The Sparkling City by the Sea!
YOU REACHED THE BEACH!
Miles to spare: 0

==================================================

==================================================

content.title: CodeGrade Expects Exact Output
content.body: CodeGrade compares your output character-by-character. Capitalization, spacing, punctuation, and exact label wording all matter. Use the Trip Summary format shown above exactly as written — including the Total Distance: label, the colon, the spacing, and any blank lines. If your code is logically correct but CodeGrade says it's failing, look for tiny differences first.

rows: Python is case-sensitive — wrong case means no match
No message for STOPPED or ERROR status

content.body: Test each status outcome:

items: if-elif-else checking status for the final message
All statuses producing an appropriate closing message

beforeYouSubmit: Docstring includes name, abc123, section, and description
Nested if inside the Houston branch
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Chapters 2 and 3: all Chapter 2 and 3 techniques including nested if statements, logical operators (or, and, not), abs(), and string comparisons. NOT permitted: loops, lists, functions you define, or any Chapter 4+ techniques. Note: The main() structure is a required template you copy exactly — you'll learn why in Chapter 5.

permittedTechniques.notPermitted: loops, lists, functions you define, or any Chapter 4+ techniques. Note: The main() structure is a required template — you'll learn why in Chapter 5.

ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP1 (Setup & New Variables) — you didn't add the new constant at the top of your file. Should be HOU_TO_GALVESTON = 50.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: NameError: name 'distance' or 'miles_remaining' is not defined in the Trip Summary
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP1 (Setup & New Variables) — you didn't initialize the tracking variables BEFORE the if-elif-else chain. They must exist before any branch references them.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Houston branch always continues to Galveston even when the player types 'n'
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP2 (Nested Houston Logic) — your or condition is wrong. The correct pattern is continue_choice == 'y' or continue_choice == 'Y'. Writing continue_choice == 'y' or 'Y' is always True (a common gotcha).

Check CP2 (Nested Houston Logic) — nested code must be indented FURTHER than the outer if. The inner if miles_remaining >= HOU_TO_GALVESTON sits inside the outer if continue_choice == 'y', so it gets two levels of indentation.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Trip Summary shows Status: STOPPED or other status but the closing message is wrong (or missing)
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP4 (Trip Summary & Final Message) — verify your final if-elif-else checks the exact uppercase status strings ('WINNER', 'SHORT', 'DETOUR') with consistent quotes. Python is case-sensitive.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Player chose Corpus, fell short, but Trip Summary shows Status: ERROR or Distance: 0
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP3 (Update Other Branches) — the Corpus branch is missing the distance or status assignment. Every branch needs to set both.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: NameError: name 'status' is not defined at the closing message
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP3 (Update Other Branches) — one of your branches (probably the else/invalid case) doesn't set status. The tracking variable defaults in CP1 should cover this, but verify the defaults are present.

ifYouGetStuck: Draw the decision tree on paper first — Houston has 4 paths, sketch them all before coding
Check your indentation carefully — nested code must be indented further than the outer if
Test one branch at a time using the debug technique from earlier checkpoints
Check Program 3-5 (loan_qualifier.py) for the nested if pattern
Submit to CodeGrade early and often to see which tests pass
